name: Microsoft SenseCam
short_description: A neck-worn passive camera with a fisheye lens and environmental sensors that automatically captures thousands of images per day, creating a visual lifelog used as a memory aid and the hardware backbone of the MyLifeBits project.
long_description: Invented by Lyndsay Williams at Microsoft Research Cambridge, SenseCam is worn on a lanyard around the neck and captures JPEG images automatically — not at fixed intervals, but whenever its sensors detect a meaningful change in the environment: a spike in light level, a shift in temperature, a body-heat signature from a person nearby, or an acceleration event. This event-triggered design means the camera is selective rather than exhaustive, yet still accumulates 2,000–4,000 images in a typical day. Reviewing the day's images compressed into a short video proved to be a powerful trigger for episodic memory recall, leading to clinical trials with Alzheimer's and amnesia patients at Addenbrooke's Hospital. The device was also adopted as the primary capture tool for Gordon Bell's MyLifeBits project — an effort to store every document, call, and experience from a human life — making SenseCam the physical embodiment of the lifelogging vision. Over 200 academic papers have been published using SenseCam data, and researcher Cathal Gurrin (Dublin City University) has worn one continuously since 2006, accumulating more than 13 million images. The technology was later licensed as the Vicon Revue and OMG Autographer.
ai_note: The capture hardware is sensor-driven with no AI; AI/ML appears downstream in event segmentation software and later clinical review tools. The device itself is a smart sensor, not an ML system.

images.caption: SenseCam prototype circa 2003, designed by Lyndsay Williams at Microsoft Research Cambridge.
images.alt_text: A small rectangular wearable camera device with a fisheye lens on the front, designed to be worn around the neck on a lanyard.

sources.title: SenseCam — Microsoft Research
sources.access_notes: Official Microsoft Research project page. Established February 25, 2004. Notes over 200 academic papers using the platform and commercialization as Vicon Revue and OMG Autographer.

sources.title: MyLifeBits — Microsoft Research
sources.access_notes: Official page for Gordon Bell's MyLifeBits project, which used SenseCam as its primary visual capture device.

sources.title: Microsoft SenseCam — Wikipedia
sources.access_notes: Secondary source covering invention history, technical specifications (fisheye lens, accelerometer, PIR, temperature sensor), clinical applications, and successor products.

interactions.title: Replay your day as a fast diary film
interactions.description: At the end of a day the wearer pulls the captured stills onto a PC and watches them play back as a sped-up sequence; Microsoft Research found that flicking through the images you took earlier tends to elicit vivid remembering of the original event.

interactions.title: Wear it as a personal 'black box'
interactions.description: SenseCam was originally conceived as a body-worn accident recorder — a personal black box that quietly logs what happened around you so the moments before an incident can be reviewed afterward.

interactions.title: Let the environment decide when to shoot
interactions.description: Instead of fixed intervals the wearer just goes about their day while the device fires the shutter on its own — a spike in light, a temperature shift, body heat from someone walking up, or a jolt of motion all trigger a frame, so a typical day fills with thousands of selectively captured images.

interactions.title: Review your week to rebuild lost memories
interactions.description: In a clinical study at Addenbrooke's Hospital, an amnesic patient (Mrs B) periodically reviewed SenseCam images of personally experienced events as a pictorial diary; after viewing them she could recall a far larger share of recent events than with a written diary, with the benefit persisting months later.

interactions.title: Feed the lifetime archive in MyLifeBits
interactions.description: Gordon Bell wore SenseCam as the visual capture front-end for MyLifeBits, the project to store everything from a person's life, so the automatically taken images flowed into a searchable lifetime database alongside documents, calls, and email.

interactions.title: Wear one for years on end as a living archive
interactions.description: Researcher Cathal Gurrin at Dublin City University has worn a lifelogging camera through nearly all his waking hours since a 2006 trip to Norway, accumulating millions of images that his group segments into events like eating and driving — a real long-term deployment of the SenseCam approach.
interactions.source_label: Science Gallery Dublin — Cathal Gurrin & DCU

interactions.title: A passive food-and-portion log for dietitians
interactions.description: Because the camera fires on its own when light or motion changes, a wearer could capture every meal and snack without remembering to record anything, giving a dietitian an unbiased picture of what and when they actually ate — grounded in SenseCam's event-triggered, hands-free capture.

interactions.title: A handover record for a shared caregiving day
interactions.description: A care worker wears the device through a shift so the next person can scroll the day's automatically captured frames to see where the client went and who visited, leaning on the same episodic-recall effect the memory studies relied on.
